era_context: Early Classical Greece; Darius I rules the Achaemenid Persian Empire. Athens is consolidating democratic reforms under Cleisthenes (implemented ~508 BCE). Sparta dominates the Peloponnese. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its final decades (ends 476 BCE). In India, the Buddha is in his final years or recently deceased (chronology highly disputed). The Mediterranean world is experiencing increased trade and colonisation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Indian subcontinent (limited written records)

events.title: Ionian Revolt begins against Persian rule
events.region: Ionia (western coast of Asia Minor), Persian Empire
events.description: The Ionian Revolt, one of the most significant rebellions against Achaemenid Persian rule, began around 499 BCE. The revolt was initiated by Aristagoras, tyrant of Miletus, who had been deposed by the Persian satrap Artaphernes. Aristagoras rallied the Ionian Greek city-states against Persian taxation and governance. The revolt quickly spread to include most of Ionia and eventually expanded to Cyprus, Aeolia, and the Hellespont. Although ultimately suppressed by 493 BCE, the Ionian Revolt was a major catalyst for the later Greco-Persian Wars (490–479 BCE).
key_figures: Histiaeus (former tyrant of Miletus, involved later)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from destroyed Ionian cities (destruction layers, artefacts)

events.certainty_note: The Ionian Revolt is well-attested in Herodotus and confirmed by archaeological evidence of destruction layers in Ionian cities. The date of 499 BCE is scholarly consensus, though some sources place the beginning in 500 BCE.
cross_references: Led to -490-001 (First Persian invasion of Greece)

events.title: Possible archonship of Callistratus in Athens
events.description: Callistratus is traditionally listed as archon of Athens in 499 BCE, though the Athenian archon list has chronological uncertainties. Little is known about his tenure. This archonship falls during the early democratic period, before the Persian Wars.

sources.name: Aristotle, Constitution of the Athenians (archon list)

events.certainty_note: The Athenian archon list is a later compilation with known chronological uncertainties of ±1–2 years. The date 499 BCE is traditional but not independently verified.

events.title: Possible death of Confucius (alternative chronology)
events.description: Some traditional Chinese chronologies place the death of Confucius (Kong Qiu) around 479 BCE, but alternative chronologies, particularly those based on earlier texts, have suggested dates as early as 499 BCE. However, the scholarly consensus strongly favours 479 BCE. This entry is included to note the chronological dispute, not to assert that Confucius died in 499 BCE.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The traditional date of Confucius' death is 479 BCE, which is the scholarly consensus. Some alternative chronologies have proposed earlier dates, but these are not widely accepted. The date 499 BCE is not a credible alternative in modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The Ionian Revolt is well-attested, but some sources place its beginning in 500 BCE rather than 499 BCE. The difference is minor and reflects uncertainty in ancient chronology. The archonship of Callistratus is listed in later sources but is not independently verified and may be subject to chronological uncertainties. The claim that Confucius died in 499 BCE is not supported by modern scholarship; the consensus date is 479 BCE. The alternative chronology suggesting 499 BCE is not credible and should not be treated as a serious historical claim.
historiographic_note: 499 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Mediterranean world, primarily through Herodotus' account of the Ionian Revolt. The Ionian Revolt is confirmed by archaeological evidence of destruction layers in Ionian cities. However, Herodotus was writing ~50 years after these events, and his account contains some legendary elements. The Athenian archon list is a later compilation with known gaps and uncertainties. For China, the Spring and Autumn period is documented in the Shiji and other texts, but these were compiled centuries after the events. For India, the record is even more problematic, with Buddhist texts compiled centuries after the Buddha's death. The historical record for 499 BCE is biased toward Greek and Mediterranean history; events in Persia, India, and China are either poorly documented or known only through later compilations.

graph_edges.note: Ionian Revolt expanded to Cyprus and Hellespont in subsequent years
Ionian Revolt led to destruction of Miletus in 494 BCE
Ionian Revolt was a major catalyst for the First Persian invasion of Greece
